problem_summary: 2013 MCM Problem A (continuous): the "Ultimate Brownie Pan." A rectangular pan over-bakes corners (heat concentrates there) while a round pan bakes evenly but wastes oven space. The task: given a pan of fixed area A and an oven of width-to-length ratio W/L with two racks, build (1) a model of heat distribution across a pan's shape, (2) a model of how many pans of a given shape fit in the oven, and (3) a combined optimizer that, for a baker's preference weight p between maximizing pan count and maximizing even heating, returns the single best pan shape. This team restricted the shape family to regular polygons (n sides) plus the circle, and delivered an algorithm that outputs the optimal n for any (W, L, A, p).
models_used: Heat-diffusion PDE model of the batter: the 2D/3D heat (diffusion) equation solved numerically by a Forward-Time Centered-Space (FTCS) finite-difference scheme on an evenly-spaced grid (21x21 horizontal, 10 vertical, 180 time steps)

Newton's Law of Heating/Cooling as a boundary/source term: applied to grid cells within a thin band Delta-w of the pan edge (pan->batter, coefficient h_pan) and to the top surface (air->batter, coefficient h_air), each scaled by local surface-area-to-volume ratio Delta-A/Delta-V
Combined governing equation: dT/dt = (K/c-rho) nabla^2 T + (h_pan/c-rho)(Delta-A/Delta-V)(T - T_pan) + (h_air/c-rho)(Delta-A/Delta-V)(T - T_air)

Geometric corner-weighting model: a closed-form weight A = 2 - sin(theta - pi/2) for the surface area assigned to corner data points, where theta = (n-2)/n * pi is the interior angle; engineered to equal 2 for a square (90 deg) and approach 1 as n->infinity (circle)

Lattice / tessellation packing model: lattice packing for polygons (using 2*apothem for multiples-of-4 sides, 2*radius x 2*apothem bounding rectangle otherwise, testing both orientations) plus explicit hexagon/square tessellation; an 8% area inflation (1.08A) injected to force airflow gaps between tessellating pans

Two normalized efficiency metrics combined by linear scalarization: Eff_space = N*A/(L*W); Eff_heating = E_min/E_max = (avg edge temperature)/(avg corner temperature); Eff_total = p*Eff_space + (1-p)*Eff_heating

Experimental parameter-ID models: calorimetry (q = c m Delta-T) for specific heat; analytic solution of Newton's cooling law T(t)=T_pan+(T_room-T_pan)exp(-(h/c-rho)(A/V)t) with least-squares fit to thermocouple time series to back out h_pan (~47-50 W/m^2C) and h_air (~8-8.8 W/m^2C)
key_techniques: Reduced the messy physics to three lumped dimensionless-ish constants alpha = K/c-rho (thermal diffusivity), beta = h_pan/c-rho, gamma = h_air/c-rho, shrinking the parameter space and simplifying the simulation code

Ran their own physical experiment: baked two batches of brownies, logged temperature vs. time with K-type thermocouples at center and edge, did a Styrofoam-cup calorimetry experiment, and measured mass/volume/density before/during/after baking

Validated experimentally-measured constants against published literature (Baik et al. 1999 cupcake thermal-property study) to confirm correct order of magnitude for density, specific heat, and especially thermal conductivity they could not measure directly

Data hygiene: identified a ~104C temperature plateau (water boiling off near the surface) and deliberately discarded post-plateau points before fitting heat-transfer coefficients, avoiding biased fits

Turned a fixed-area constraint into an apothem/radius geometry problem and discovered 'magic numbers' - oven dimensions W=L=2*apothem*k (multiple-of-4 polygons) or W=2r*j, L=2R*k (other even polygons) that produce local maxima in packing efficiency

Used a tally/voting scheme across a sweep of pan areas: for each weight p they counted how many areas each shape 'won' (Figure 7 table), giving a robust shape recommendation rather than relying on a single area
Defined heating efficiency as a self-normalizing ratio (edge temp / corner temp) bounded in [0,1] so it sits on the same 0-1 scale as space efficiency and can be linearly weighted with no rescaling
why_it_won: From a judge's seat, this paper wins on a rare combination: it answered the actual question literally and completely (an algorithm that takes W, L, A, p and returns the optimal shape), and it backed its thermodynamics with a real, hands-on experiment instead of pulling constants from thin air. The experimental layer is the differentiator - most teams assume a heat-transfer coefficient; this team baked brownies, instrumented them with thermocouples, ran a calorimetry experiment, fit the data with least squares, and then cross-checked their numbers against a peer-reviewed food-science paper. That is exactly the empirical credibility judges reward. The corner-weighting formula A = 2 - sin(theta - pi/2) shows genuine modeling craft: they hit a concrete numerical obstacle (grid cells unevenly catching pan edge), reasoned about what physically matters (vertex angle), and invented a clean closed-form weight with the correct limiting behavior (2 at a square corner, 1 at a circle). The two sub-models (packing and heating) are cleanly separated, each independently defensible, and fused by a transparent weighted-sum the customer controls. The sensitivity analysis is unusually thorough - they perturbed every constant (alpha, beta, gamma, c-rho, temperature, height, time, area, n) over orders of magnitude, reported worst-case efficiency deltas, and explained which parameters actually matter (h_pan and c-rho) versus which don't (gamma, alpha). The honest, non-dramatic central finding - "there is no one optimal pan; run the algorithm per situation" - reads as mature modeling rather than overclaiming. They even closed the loop on practicality: 'magic numbers' tell a manufacturer which oven/pan dimensions hit packing sweet spots.
section_highlights: The paper opens with a one-page customer-facing brochure for fictional "Polythermal Bakeware" - a memorable framing device that restates the whole solution in plain language before the technical report, signaling they understood this was an applied, communicate-to-a-baker problem. The Assumptions section (9 numbered items) is strong: each assumption is paired with an explicit physical justification (oven as heat reservoir, pan reaches oven temp fast, vertical-only batter expansion so horizontal heat distribution is unaffected), and several are revisited later as acknowledged weaknesses. The "Determining the surface area of each data point" subsection is the modeling centerpiece - it narrates three successive attempts (raw grid overlay -> uniform averaging -> hybrid edge/corner weighting) and explains why each earlier approach failed, which reads as authentic scientific reasoning rather than a polished-after-the-fact story. The experimental section with actual temperature-vs-time tables (Figures 5 and 6), the 104C plateau discovery, and the literature comparison to Baik et al. is the empirical anchor. The sensitivity analysis spans roughly six subsections with four line-chart figures (efficiency vs. polygon sides across alpha, beta, gamma, and c-rho sweeps), each with a quantified worst-case delta and a physical interpretation. Strengths/Weaknesses/Future-Improvements are candid and specific (e.g., admitting a radial discretization might capture features their Cartesian grid missed, and that the corner-area assignment was not rigorously proven). The conclusion's aphorism - "For every customer there is one optimal pan, but there is no one optimal pan for every customer" - lands the whole argument memorably.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Shape family is artificially narrow: only regular polygons + circle. Real bakeware (rounded-corner rectangles, ellipses, superellipses/'squircles') was excluded, and the squircle is arguably the true intended answer to this problem - they acknowledge this only briefly at the end

Two inconsistent definitions of heating efficiency appear in the paper: the narrative defines it as edge-temp/corner-temp (Eff_heating), but Equation 11 writes it as E_min/E_max = 'least-heated edge area / most-heated edge area' - the metric is described two different ways and never fully reconciled

Heat-transfer coefficients rest on very few data points: after discarding post-plateau (>104C) data they had only 3 edge points and 4 center points; they themselves call this an 'order-of-magnitude estimate', so the entire heating model is calibrated on thin data
An obvious unit error: the procedure states brownies were baked at '350C' (almost certainly 350F / ~177C) - an uncaught typo in the experimental write-up that a judge would notice

The 8% (1.08A) area-inflation for airflow is an arbitrary, unjustified fudge factor applied only to squares and hexagons - no derivation, no sensitivity check on this specific number, and it directly affects the packing-efficiency comparison

The second baking experiment (three threaded thermocouples) failed - heat conducted down the wires inflated readings - so that data was thrown out; the model never got the cleaner dataset it was designed to collect

Top/bottom-of-pan heating is deliberately ignored by measuring only the 'middle layer'; the height-sensitivity result (5.5% swing between 1in and 6in) shows this simplification materially affects the efficiency number

Modeling shortcuts compound: density and mass were measured to change substantially during baking (1040->840 kg/m^3) but were frozen as constants 'to make the code simpler', and water evaporation / batter restructuring at high temperature are entirely omitted

FTCS stability is treated empirically, not analytically: they note the scheme 'failed to converge' above certain alpha and beta values but never state or check the CFL/von Neumann stability condition that explains it, so the safe operating envelope is found by trial and error
reusable_patterns: Linear-scalarization of competing objectives onto a shared [0,1] scale: Eff_total = p*Eff_A + (1-p)*Eff_B, with both metrics self-normalized so the weight p is directly interpretable as the stakeholder's preference - a clean, judge-friendly multi-objective pattern

Separate-then-fuse architecture: split a problem into independent sub-models (here packing vs. heating), make each defensible on its own, then combine with a transparent weighting - easier to validate and to write up than a monolithic model

Calibrate-then-validate-against-literature loop: measure physical constants yourself experimentally, then benchmark them against a published peer-reviewed study to establish order-of-magnitude credibility - powerful when you lack precise equipment

Lump compound physical parameters into single effective constants (alpha=K/c-rho, beta=h/c-rho) to shrink the parameter space, simplify simulation, and make sensitivity analysis cleaner (you only perturb the lumped quantity that actually enters the equations)

Engineer a closed-form weighting function to satisfy stated limit conditions: write down the boundary behavior you need (= 2 at theta=90deg, -> 1 as theta->180deg) and construct a simple trig/algebraic formula that meets them - a transferable trick for ad-hoc geometric/physical weights

Sweep-and-tally robustness check: instead of reporting one optimum, run the optimizer across a range of a free parameter (pan area) and count 'wins' per option to surface a recommendation stable to that parameter (Figure 7 voting table)

Discover and exploit 'resonance' / sweet-spot dimensions: derive the input values (here W=L=2*apothem*k) that produce local efficiency maxima and report them as actionable design guidance ('magic numbers')

Honest data-trimming with a stated physical reason: detect a regime change in your data (the 104C boiling plateau), explain the mechanism, and exclude the contaminated points before fitting - and document that you did it

Per-parameter sensitivity table with worst-case deltas and physical interpretation: vary each constant over orders of magnitude, report the maximum efficiency change, and say which parameters dominate - a template for a rigorous, compact sensitivity section

Front-load a plain-language customer/executive artifact (the brochure) before the technical report so non-expert readers grasp the solution immediately - strong for applied/communication-graded problems
